walkthrough.title: Gather Your Materials
walkthrough.text: Let's get Boggle set up. First, make sure everyone at the table has a sheet of paper and a pencil or pen. You will need these to write down words during each round, and you want to keep your list hidden from other players.

walkthrough.title: Load the Dice into the Tray
walkthrough.text: Find the sixteen letter dice and the four-by-four plastic grid tray. Drop all sixteen dice into the tray so each die sits in its own slot. One of the dice has a face that shows "Qu" instead of a single letter, which is totally normal.

walkthrough.title: Cover and Shake
walkthrough.text: Snap the clear plastic dome cover over the tray. Now give it a good vigorous shake to randomize all the dice. Set the tray down on the table and let the dice settle into their grid positions.

walkthrough.title: Reveal the Grid
walkthrough.text: Lift the dome off the tray so everyone can see the four-by-four letter grid. Place the tray in the center of the table where all players can read it. Make sure nobody starts looking for words yet.

walkthrough.title: Set the Timer
walkthrough.text: Place the three-minute sand timer where everyone can see it. One player will flip it to start each round. Once the timer is flipped, everyone searches for words at the same time, so make sure everyone is ready before you start.

summary.title: Gather Your Materials
bullets: Each player needs paper and a pencil/pen
Lists are private — hide from other players

summary.title: Load the Dice into the Tray
bullets: Place all 16 letter dice into the 4x4 grid tray
One die has a "Qu" face — counts as 2 letters

summary.title: Cover and Shake
bullets: Snap dome cover over the tray
Shake vigorously → set down → let dice settle

summary.title: Reveal the Grid
bullets: Remove dome, place tray centrally
All players must be able to read the grid

summary.title: Set the Timer
bullets: Place 3-min sand timer where all can see
One player flips it to start — everyone plays simultaneously

walkthrough.title: How a Round Works
walkthrough.text: Boggle is played in rounds. Each round, you shake the grid to get a fresh set of letters, flip the three-minute timer, and everyone searches for words at the same time. When the timer runs out, all pencils go down immediately. There are no turns in Boggle because everyone plays simultaneously.

walkthrough.title: How to Form Words
walkthrough.text: Words are formed by connecting adjacent dice in the grid. Adjacent means directly next to each other horizontally, vertically, or diagonally. Each letter in your word must be touching the previous letter, creating a connected chain through the grid.

walkthrough.title: The Rules for Valid Words
walkthrough.text: Every word must be at least three letters long. You can only use each die once per word, so you cannot revisit a die you already used in the same chain. Words must be real English dictionary words, and proper nouns like names of people or places are not allowed.

walkthrough.title: The Qu Die
walkthrough.text: One of the dice has a face that shows "Qu" instead of a single letter. When you use this die, it counts as two letters in your word. So a word like QUIT spelled with the Qu die plus I and T uses three dice but counts as a four-letter word for scoring.

walkthrough.title: Comparing Lists and Eliminating Duplicates
walkthrough.text: After time runs out, everyone reads their word list out loud. If two or more players wrote down the same word, all of those players cross it out. Nobody scores for a shared word. Only words that appear on exactly one player's list count for points.

walkthrough.title: Challenging Words
walkthrough.text: Any player can challenge a word they think is not real. Use a dictionary to settle the dispute. If the word is not in the dictionary, it scores zero points. Players can also challenge whether a valid path exists for the word on the grid.

walkthrough.title: Scoring
walkthrough.text: After eliminating duplicates and resolving challenges, score your remaining unique words by length. Three and four-letter words are worth one point each. Five-letter words are worth two points. Six-letter words are worth three points. Seven-letter words are worth five points, and eight-letter words or longer are worth eleven points.

walkthrough.title: Winning the Game
walkthrough.text: The player with the most total points wins. You can play a single round as a complete game, or play multiple rounds and add up scores across all of them. If there is a tie, play one more round to break it or share the victory.

bullets: Shake grid → flip 3-min timer → everyone searches simultaneously
Timer expires → all pencils down immediately
No turns — all players play at the same time

walkthrough.title: Welcome to Practice
walkthrough.text: Welcome to your first round of Boggle. We are going to walk through a practice round together, showing you common mistakes and how to avoid them. Even experienced word-game players fall into these traps, so follow along closely.

walkthrough.title: The Grid Is Revealed
walkthrough.text: We have just shaken the tray and revealed our four-by-four letter grid. Before the timer starts, take a quick scan of the whole grid. Notice where the vowels are, because every word needs at least one. Locate any high-value letter clusters where vowels sit next to groups of consonants.

walkthrough.title: Mistake: Only Finding Short Common Words
walkthrough.text: The timer starts and Player A immediately writes down every three-letter word they spot. THE, AND, FOR, HAS, RAN. They fill their list fast and feel great about it. But here is the problem: those are the most obvious words on the grid, and every other player is finding them too.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Short Common Words Get Eliminated
walkthrough.text: When we compare lists, every short common word Player A found was also on two other lists. All of them get crossed out and score zero. Three-letter words are worth only one point each, and they are the most likely to be duplicated. The lesson is to hunt for longer and less obvious words first, then fill in short words with leftover time.

walkthrough.title: Mistake: Missing Diagonal Connections
walkthrough.text: Player B is scanning the grid but only looking at letters that are directly above, below, left, or right of each other. They completely miss that the S in the top-right corner connects diagonally to the T below-left, which connects to an A, forming STAR. They found half the words they could have.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Always Check All Eight Directions
walkthrough.text: Every die in the grid connects to up to eight neighbors, not just four. Diagonal connections are where the best words hide because other players often overlook them too. When you find a promising starting letter, check all eight surrounding dice before moving on.

walkthrough.title: Mistake: Reusing a Die in a Word Path
walkthrough.text: Player C spots what looks like the word LETTER on the grid. They trace L-E-T-T-E-R, but the grid only has one T in that area. They used the same T die twice in their path. They write it down anyway, hoping nobody notices.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Trace Your Path Carefully
walkthrough.text: During the comparison phase, another player challenges LETTER and asks Player C to trace the path. Player C cannot show a valid chain without reusing the T die, so the word scores zero. Before writing any word down, mentally trace the full path and make sure each die is used only once.

walkthrough.title: Mistake: Camping in One Corner
walkthrough.text: Player D found a great cluster of letters in the bottom-left corner and spent the entire three minutes mining words from those six dice. They found eight words from that corner, but they completely ignored the other twelve dice on the grid. Meanwhile, other players found fifteen or twenty words across the full grid.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Scan the Whole Grid Systematically
walkthrough.text: It is tempting to stay in a productive area, but you are leaving points on the table. Work through the grid in a pattern. Start in one corner, sweep across, then move down. Spend no more than thirty to forty-five seconds in any one area before moving on to fresh territory.

walkthrough.title: Mistake: Ignoring the Qu Die
walkthrough.text: Player A sees the Qu die sitting on the grid and skips right past it. They assume Q words are too hard or too rare to bother with. But QUIT, QUAIL, QUEST, and QUEEN are all common words, and the Qu die hands you two letters for free.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: The Qu Die Is Your Friend
walkthrough.text: The Qu die is actually a gift because it gives you two letters from one die, making your word longer with fewer dice needed. A three-die word using the Qu die counts as four letters and scores the same as any other four-letter word. Always check what vowels and consonants surround the Qu die.

walkthrough.title: Putting It All Together
walkthrough.text: Let's replay the round with good habits. Start by scanning the whole grid for long words and unusual combinations. Check all eight directions around each promising letter. Trace your path mentally before writing anything down. Move through the grid systematically, and do not forget to check the Qu die.

walkthrough.title: Before You Play
walkthrough.text:
Before you dive into a real game, here are three things that can help.

First, check out General Tips for scanning strategies and time management advice.

Second, reference the Appendix for a quick guide to scoring, word rules, and grid mechanics.

And most importantly, ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Have fun!

Reference the Appendix → scoring, word rules, grid mechanics
Ask your personal GM Guide → tap Q&A for instant answers during play

walkthrough.title: Hunt Long Words First
walkthrough.text: The single biggest scoring tip in Boggle is to prioritize long words. An eight-letter word is worth eleven points, which equals eleven three-letter words. Longer words are also far less likely to appear on another player's list, so they survive the duplicate elimination phase. Spend your first sixty seconds scanning for five-letter words and above.

walkthrough.title: Use Prefixes and Suffixes
walkthrough.text: Look for common word endings like ING, ED, ER, EST, and TION on the grid. If you spot an ING cluster, work backwards from it to find every word that ends in ING. Similarly, look for prefixes like UN, RE, and PRE. These letter patterns unlock longer words that less experienced players miss entirely.

walkthrough.title: Anchor on Vowels
walkthrough.text: Every word needs at least one vowel, so vowels are your starting points. Find each vowel on the grid and examine the consonants surrounding it. A vowel surrounded by four or five consonants is a gold mine because it can be the hub of many different words radiating outward.

walkthrough.title: Write First, Verify Later
walkthrough.text: Do not waste precious seconds during the three-minute timer debating whether a word is real. If it looks like it could be a word, write it down and keep scanning. You can always cross out a bad word during the comparison phase, but you cannot get back time you spent hesitating.

walkthrough.title: Think Like Your Opponents
walkthrough.text: The duplicate elimination rule means that finding words nobody else found is more valuable than finding the most words. If a word jumps out at you instantly, it probably jumped out at everyone else too. When you spot an obvious word, write it down quickly and move on to hunt for less obvious ones.

walkthrough.title: Manage Your Time
walkthrough.text: Three minutes goes fast. A good split is sixty seconds hunting for long words, sixty seconds doing a systematic sweep of the full grid, and sixty seconds filling in any short words you missed. Glance at the sand timer periodically so you do not get caught with time running out and half the grid unscanned.

walkthrough.title: Practice Grid Reading
walkthrough.text: The best Boggle players develop a knack for reading letter grids quickly. You can practice between games by looking at any grid of letters, such as a license plate or a sign, and mentally forming words. The more you practice scanning letter patterns, the faster your brain will spot words during the timed round.

walkthrough.title: Stay Calm Under Pressure
walkthrough.text: Boggle is a real-time game and the timer creates pressure. Some players panic and start scribbling every two-letter combination they see, even though two-letter words do not count. Take a breath, focus on the grid, and trust your scanning strategy. Calm methodical searching beats frantic scrambling every time.

walkthrough.title: The Obscure Word Advantage
walkthrough.text: Advanced Boggle is won by finding words that no one else finds. Build your vocabulary with uncommon but valid words like NAVE, ERNE, TARN, BOLE, and NARD. These short obscure words fly under other players' radar and survive the duplicate elimination. A player who finds ten unique obscure words will outscore someone with twenty common words that all get crossed out.

walkthrough.title: Reverse Engineering Words
walkthrough.text: Instead of scanning the grid letter by letter, think of words you know and check if they exist on the grid. Experienced players keep a mental list of high-value words and quickly check for them each round. This is especially powerful for six-letter words and above where the point payoff is massive.

walkthrough.title: Maximizing the Qu Die
walkthrough.text: The Qu die is an advanced player's secret weapon. Because it gives you two letters from one die, it effectively shortens the path you need to trace. A five-letter Qu word only requires four dice on the grid. Keep a mental list of Qu words like QUAFF, QUALM, QUASH, QUOTA, and SQUAT ready for any round where the Qu die appears.

walkthrough.title: Letter Frequency Awareness
walkthrough.text: The sixteen dice in Boggle are not random. They are designed with weighted letter distributions that favor common English letters. Knowing which letters appear most often helps you predict what words are likely available. E, A, I, O, S, R, N, and T are the most frequent, so practice finding words built from these letters.

walkthrough.title: Chain Branching
walkthrough.text: When you find a word, ask yourself if it branches into a longer word. If you found RAIN, check if RAINS, TRAIN, or BRAIN exists by extending the path in either direction. One discovered word can lead to three or four related words through branching. This technique multiplies your output without extra scanning time.

walkthrough.title: The Duplicate Prediction Game
walkthrough.text: Before the comparison phase, mentally rank which of your words are most likely shared by others. The words that jumped out at you first probably jumped out at everyone. When you have limited time left, invest it in hunting for words that feel unusual or require diagonal paths. Those less obvious words are your real point generators.

walkthrough.title: Multi-Round Score Management
walkthrough.text: In a multi-round game, consistency matters more than one big round. Track your scoring patterns across rounds to identify weaknesses. If you keep losing points to duplicates, focus more on obscure words. If you keep getting challenged, tighten your vocabulary accuracy. Adjust your strategy round by round based on what you learn about your opponents' tendencies.

walkthrough.title: Speed Scanning Technique
walkthrough.text: Elite players scan the grid in a specific pattern. Start at the top-left die and mentally radiate outward, checking every path of three, four, and five letters from that starting point. Then move to the next die and repeat. This systematic radial scan ensures you never miss an adjacent connection. With practice, you can cover the full sixteen-die grid in under ninety seconds.

10 unique obscure words > 20 common duplicated words

entries.definition: Two dice are adjacent if they are directly next to each other horizontally, vertically, or diagonally. Each die in the interior of the grid has eight neighbors. Corner dice have three neighbors and edge dice have five.
The chain of connected adjacent dice that spells out a word. Each die in the path must be adjacent to the previous die, and no die may be used more than once in a single word path.
After the timer expires, all players compare word lists. Any word found by two or more players is crossed out for everyone. Only words unique to a single player's list score points.
All players search for words at the same time during the three-minute timer. There are no turns or player order in the word-finding phase.
Words must be at least three letters long to be valid. Two-letter combinations do not count even if they form real words.
Names of people, places, brands, and other proper nouns are not valid Boggle words. Only common English dictionary words count.
One die face shows "Qu" instead of a single letter. It counts as two letters but occupies one grid space. Words using the Qu die add both Q and U to the letter count for scoring purposes.
Conjugated verb forms such as RUNS and RUNNING are valid. Standard plural forms are also valid. These count as separate words from their base form.
Any player may challenge a word's validity or its grid path after the timer expires. Use a dictionary to resolve word disputes. An invalid word scores zero points.

Three-letter and four-letter words score one point. Five-letter words score two points. Six-letter words score three points. Seven-letter words score five points. Words of eight or more letters score eleven points.
A word that appears on only one player's list after the comparison phase. Only unique words contribute to a player's score for the round.
When playing multiple rounds, add each round's score to a cumulative total. The player with the highest cumulative score at the end of all rounds wins.
The four-by-four plastic tray that holds all sixteen letter dice in a grid formation. Covered with a dome for shaking and placed centrally so all players can read it.
A three-minute hourglass timer used to limit each round. When the sand runs out, all players must stop writing immediately.
Sixteen six-sided dice with letters printed on each face. The letter distribution is weighted toward common English letters to produce playable grids. Each die has a unique set of six letters.
